title: ECG — Performing
oneLiner: Standardised 12-lead recording of cardiac electrical activity; essential for arrhythmia, ischaemia, and structural heart disease diagnosis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Unstable arrhythmia (VT, VF, severe bradycardia):** do NOT delay defibrillation, cardioversion, or transcutaneous pacing to obtain a 12-lead ECG. If patient unstable, treat first — obtain rhythm strip after stabilisation.  
  - *Single finding:* absence of palpable pulses + wide QRS on monitor → immediate shock, not ECG.
- **Misplaced leads mimicking STEMI or ischaemia:** lead reversal (especially limb leads) can produce false ST elevation, abnormal R-wave progression, or axis shifts that simulate acute MI.  
  - *Single finding:* unusual QRS morphology in aVR (e.g., dominant R wave) or loss of R-wave progression that doesn't match clinical picture → check lead placement before calling STEMI alert.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (indications for performing ECG):** chest pain, syncope, palpitations, pre-syncope, dyspnoea, suspected arrhythmia, pre-operative assessment, new-onset confusion, cardiac arrest (post-ROSC), monitoring of known cardiac disease.
- **Key exam findings:** none specific to the procedure itself; patient should be calm, supine (or semi-recumbent if unable), arms relaxed, chest exposed.
sections.Differential Diagnosis *(of ECG performa:
- **Most common error → less common:**
  - Limb lead reversal (most frequent)
  - Chest lead misplacement (V1 and V2 too high, V5/V6 too lateral)
  - Poor skin contact → artefact mimicking arrhythmia or ST changes
  - Machine calibration errors (double standard, wrong paper speed)
- **Splitter:** one bedside test to differentiate lead reversal from true pathology – check aVR: if P wave and QRS are upright in aVR, suspect lead reversal (normal aVR has predominantly negative QRS and inverted P wave).
sections.Investigations *(for verifying ECG quali:
- **Bedside:** inspect each lead for baseline wander, artefact, correct calibration (10 mm = 1 mV), and paper speed (25 mm/s standard). Ensure at least 6 leads are clearly visible.
- **Bloods:** none required for ECG performance; if indicated for the clinical scenario (e.g., troponin, potassium, calcium).
- **Imaging:** none.
- **Special tests:** none.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** standard ECG quality criteria (no missing leads, no excessive artefact, clear P waves and QRS in at least 3 limb leads and all chest leads). For time-critical ECGs (e.g., STEMI), aim for ≤10 minutes from arrival.
- **Never-miss test:** verifying limb lead placement (especially right arm/left arm) – omission can cause missed or false STEMI diagnosis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm indication and patient safety (no visible defibrillator pads? Remove if present, but do not delay ECG for implanted devices – place electrodes away from device pocket).
  - Apply electrodes in correct anatomical positions:
    - **Limb leads:** RA – right arm (near wrist, medial), LA – left arm, RL – right leg (ground), LL – left leg (above ankle).
    - **Chest leads:**
      - V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
      - V2: 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
      - V3: midway between V2 and V4
      - V4: 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line
      - V5: anterior axillary line, same horizontal level as V4
      - V6: midaxillary line, same horizontal level as V4
  - Ensure skin is clean, dry, and shaved if excessive hair (use paper tape or gel pads if needed).
  - Set machine: paper speed 25 mm/s, standard calibration 10 mm/mV. Record at least 10 seconds of all 12 leads + rhythm strip.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For serial ECGs (e.g., evolving ACS), mark electrode positions with a permanent marker to ensure consistency.
  - Document time, symptoms at time of recording, and any drug administration prior to ECG.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** none.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – ECG is a diagnostic tool, not a treatment. However, if patient has ongoing chest pain, order repeat ECG in 30–60 minutes if first non-diagnostic.
sections.Drugs & Doses: - None – ECG performing does not involve drugs. For specific indications (e.g., adenosine for SVT during ECG), refer to local policy.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Skin irritation/allergy to electrode gel or adhesive.
  - Burns (rare) – avoid if patient has transdermal patches (remove before electrode placement).
  - Pain from electrode removal (especially in frail elderly or thin skin).
- **Chronic:** none.
sections.Prognosis: - Prognosis relates to underlying condition, not to performance of ECG. A correctly performed ECG yields diagnostic data; a poor-quality ECG delays diagnosis and management.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use anatomical landmarks, not previous electrode locations** – especially in V1/V2 (the sternal angle landmark for 2nd intercostal space, then count down 2 spaces).
- **For female patients:** place chest electrodes *under* breast tissue, not on top, to avoid artefact and attenuation of R waves.
- **Check for artefact before declaring an arrhythmia** – lead motion (tremor, hiccups) can mimic atrial fibrillation or ventricular ectopy. Assess the baseline – if it's wandering, fix it first.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on machine interpretation** – automated algorithms have poor specificity; always review the raw tracing.
- **Do not record ECG while patient is moving or talking** – muscle artefact can obscure ST segments and simulate ischaemia.
- **Do not forget to check paper speed and calibration** – a double-standard (20 mm/mV) can falsely amplify ST elevation; incorrect paper speed (50 mm/s) will give misleading intervals.
sections.Safety Netting *(If Patient Goes Home –:
- **Return if:** chest pain recurs, new shortness of breath, palpitations (especially if rapid or irregular), or syncope.
- **Follow-up:** arrange outpatient clinic (cardiology or GP) within 1–2 weeks if symptoms were suspicious but ECG normal; document advice given.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team if:** patient develops haemodynamic instability during ECG (e.g., syncope, hypotension, ongoing chest pain with ST elevation). **Do not leave patient unattended** if they are high-risk.
- **Technical issues:** if ECG machine fails or leads produce inexplicable results, escalate to ECG technician (if available) or use a portable monitor with rhythm strip. Do not rely on a single poor-quality recording.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 during ECG (especially respiratory rate >24 or oxygen saturations <90%) → urgent clinical review before completing ECG.

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Performing
**One-liner:** Standardised 12-lead recording of cardiac electrical activity; essential for arrhythmia, ischaemia, and structural heart disease diagnosis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Unstable arrhythmia (VT, VF, severe bradycardia):** do NOT delay defibrillation, cardioversion, or transcutaneous pacing to obtain a 12-lead ECG. If patient unstable, treat first — obtain rhythm strip after stabilisation.  
  - *Single finding:* absence of palpable pulses + wide QRS on monitor → immediate shock, not ECG.
- **Misplaced leads mimicking STEMI or ischaemia:** lead reversal (especially limb leads) can produce false ST elevation, abnormal R-wave progression, or axis shifts that simulate acute MI.  
  - *Single finding:* unusual QRS morphology in aVR (e.g., dominant R wave) or loss of R-wave progression that doesn't match clinical picture → check lead placement before calling STEMI alert.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (indications for performing ECG):** chest pain, syncope, palpitations, pre-syncope, dyspnoea, suspected arrhythmia, pre-operative assessment, new-onset confusion, cardiac arrest (post-ROSC), monitoring of known cardiac disease.
- **Key exam findings:** none specific to the procedure itself; patient should be calm, supine (or semi-recumbent if unable), arms relaxed, chest exposed.

### Differential Diagnosis *(of ECG performance errors – not clinical differential)*
- **Most common error → less common:**
  - Limb lead reversal (most frequent)
  - Chest lead misplacement (V1 and V2 too high, V5/V6 too lateral)
  - Poor skin contact → artefact mimicking arrhythmia or ST changes
  - Machine calibration errors (double standard, wrong paper speed)
- **Splitter:** one bedside test to differentiate lead reversal from true pathology – check aVR: if P wave and QRS are upright in aVR, suspect lead reversal (normal aVR has predominantly negative QRS and inverted P wave).

### Investigations *(for verifying ECG quality)*
- **Bedside:** inspect each lead for baseline wander, artefact, correct calibration (10 mm = 1 mV), and paper speed (25 mm/s standard). Ensure at least 6 leads are clearly visible.
- **Bloods:** none required for ECG performance; if indicated for the clinical scenario (e.g., troponin, potassium, calcium).
- **Imaging:** none.
- **Special tests:** none.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** standard ECG quality criteria (no missing leads, no excessive artefact, clear P waves and QRS in at least 3 limb leads and all chest leads). For time-critical ECGs (e.g., STEMI), aim for ≤10 minutes from arrival.
- **Never-miss test:** verifying limb lead placement (especially right arm/left arm) – omission can cause missed or false STEMI diagnosis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm indication and patient safety (no visible defibrillator pads? Remove if present, but do not delay ECG for implanted devices – place electrodes away from device pocket).
  - Apply electrodes in correct anatomical positions:
    - **Limb leads:** RA – right arm (near wrist, medial), LA – left arm, RL – right leg (ground), LL – left leg (above ankle).
    - **Chest leads:**
      - V1: 4th intercostal space, right sternal border
      - V2: 4th intercostal space, left sternal border
      - V3: midway between V2 and V4
      - V4: 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line
      - V5: anterior axillary line, same horizontal level as V4
      - V6: midaxillary line, same horizontal level as V4
  - Ensure skin is clean, dry, and shaved if excessive hair (use paper tape or gel pads if needed).
  - Set machine: paper speed 25 mm/s, standard calibration 10 mm/mV. Record at least 10 seconds of all 12 leads + rhythm strip.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - For serial ECGs (e.g., evolving ACS), mark electrode positions with a permanent marker to ensure consistency.
  - Document time, symptoms at time of recording, and any drug administration prior to ECG.
- **Long-term / outpatient:** none.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** N/A – ECG is a diagnostic tool, not a treatment. However, if patient has ongoing chest pain, order repeat ECG in 30–60 minutes if first non-diagnostic.

### Drugs & Doses
- None – ECG performing does not involve drugs. For specific indications (e.g., adenosine for SVT during ECG), refer to local policy.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Skin irritation/allergy to electrode gel or adhesive.
  - Burns (rare) – avoid if patient has transdermal patches (remove before electrode placement).
  - Pain from electrode removal (especially in frail elderly or thin skin).
- **Chronic:** none.

### Prognosis
- Prognosis relates to underlying condition, not to performance of ECG. A correctly performed ECG yields diagnostic data; a poor-quality ECG delays diagnosis and management.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use anatomical landmarks, not previous electrode locations** – especially in V1/V2 (the sternal angle landmark for 2nd intercostal space, then count down 2 spaces).
- **For female patients:** place chest electrodes *under* breast tissue, not on top, to avoid artefact and attenuation of R waves.
- **Check for artefact before declaring an arrhythmia** – lead motion (tremor, hiccups) can mimic atrial fibrillation or ventricular ectopy. Assess the baseline – if it's wandering, fix it first.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on machine interpretation** – automated algorithms have poor specificity; always review the raw tracing.
- **Do not record ECG while patient is moving or talking** – muscle artefact can obscure ST segments and simulate ischaemia.
- **Do not forget to check paper speed and calibration** – a double-standard (20 mm/mV) can falsely amplify ST elevation; incorrect paper speed (50 mm/s) will give misleading intervals.

### Safety Netting *(If Patient Goes Home – e.g., after normal ECG)*
- **Return if:** chest pain recurs, new shortness of breath, palpitations (especially if rapid or irregular), or syncope.
- **Follow-up:** arrange outpatient clinic (cardiology or GP) within 1–2 weeks if symptoms were suspicious but ECG normal; document advice given.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / cardiac arrest team if:** patient develops haemodynamic instability during ECG (e.g., syncope, hypotension, ongoing chest pain with ST elevation). **Do not leave patient unattended** if they are high-risk.
- **Technical issues:** if ECG machine fails or leads produce inexplicable results, escalate to ECG technician (if available) or use a portable monitor with rhythm strip. Do not rely on a single poor-quality recording.
- **Specific thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5 during ECG (especially respiratory rate >24 or oxygen saturations <90%) → urgent clinical review before completing ECG.
